title: Berry smoothie
subtitle: Frozen mixed berries blended with banana, oat milk, and honey.
excerpt: Frozen mixed berries, a banana, oat milk, and honey blended until smooth. Ready in two minutes. Thick enough to eat with a spoon or thin enough to drink through a straw depending on the milk quantity.
sourceNotes: Blended fruit drinks have been made at home since domestic blenders became affordable in the mid-twentieth century. The modern smoothie culture in Britain developed from the late 1990s, partly through juice bars and partly through the commercial success of brands selling bottled smoothies from the early 2000s. The frozen berry and banana combination became standard because frozen fruit produces a thicker, colder result than fresh fruit.

content.text: Frozen berries give a better result than fresh ones in a smoothie. They are colder, which keeps the drink thick and cold without needing ice (which dilutes), and they are available year-round. Bags of frozen mixed berries are substantially cheaper than fresh.
The banana is the thickener. Without it, a berry-and-milk smoothie is thin and watery. One banana per two servings is about right; more produces a drink that tastes more of banana than berries.
A high-powered blender. A stick blender can work with softer fruit but may struggle with frozen berries.

Put {{mixed-frozen-berries}} of frozen berries, the banana, {{oat-milk}} of oat milk, and {{honey}} of honey into the blender. Add the yoghurt if using. Blend on high for 30 to 45 seconds until completely smooth. Taste and adjust the honey. Add more milk for a thinner consistency. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Leave the berries out for 5 minutes to start thawing, or add the milk first so it cushions the blades before the fruit.
items.cause: Frozen berries can seize a standard blender.
items.symptom: Blender is struggling with frozen fruit

items.fix: Add more frozen berries or another piece of banana and blend again. More frozen fruit always thickens.
items.cause: Too much milk or the banana was very ripe and watery.

items.fix: Add more honey or an extra half banana to balance the sharpness.
items.cause: The berry mix contains blackcurrants or blackberries, which are more acidic than strawberries or raspberries.

items.fix: Strain through a fine-mesh sieve for a smoother result if you find the seeds unpleasant.
items.cause: Raspberries and blackberries have small seeds that blenders may not fully break down.

content.text: The smoothie in its current form grew out of British juice bars in the late 1990s. By the early 2000s, high street chains sold them as breakfast in a bottle. Home cooks copied the idea once blenders became standard. Frozen berry and banana is now the most common home version. It is cheap, and it tastes the same all year round, which a fresh fruit version cannot do.
